dialogue.utterance: Medical alarm dispatched to 78 year old male in Richmond with trouble breathing.
Hi, I'm Maya, an EMT. What made you call 911 today, sir?
Can't breathe. Like all morning, but worse now for about two hours.
Sir, can you follow my finger with both eyes?
You're responding with both eyes and tracking well. Good eye contact.
Can you tell me your name for me?
Can you squeeze my finger with your right hand? Left hand too?
Your airway is clear, but I see some frothy sputum. Do you have a history of heart failure, stroke, or seizures?
Respirations 29 a minute, SpO₂ is 95%.
You're breathing quickly and having retractions, especially underneath your sternum. Breathing seems labored.
Heart rate is 140, systolic BP is 214, pulses are strong and regular.
The skin is warm, dry, and normal in color.
Can you tell me about your medical history and any recent changes?
I have CHF, had a stroke, and seizures. I was recently in the hospital.
Are you on any medications or have diabetes or high cholesterol?
I have diabetes but no insulin and high cholesterol. They told me to stop most of my meds when I left the hospital.
Any allergies to medications or foods?
I'm not allergic to anything.
Do you have any chest, abdominal, or other pain?
I don't have pain, just trouble breathing.
Eyes track normally. Positive JVD noted. Neck appears normal, without masses or injuries.
Frothy nasal secretions collected and cleared. No other drainage observed.
Breath sounds on the right are diminished, with rhonchi noted in the upper lobes.
Both lungs have rhonchi. Substernal retractions noted during breathing.
Given your breathing difficulty, CHF, stroke history including CVA, and seizures, we're concerned about pulmonary edema.
Heart rate is stable at 140.
Respiration remains tachypneic at 27 per minute, shallow.
Blood pressure is now 246 over 143.
Oximetry has increased again to 97%.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia.
Glucose not prioritized just now. We note your hyperlipidemia but are focusing on breathing and cardiac function.
We're going to administer albuterol and ipratropium via nebulizer for breathing support. Okay?
Please, go ahead.
Nebulizer will help open up your airways. You may feel a slight coolness on your face. Just breathe slowly.
I'm putting you on the CPAP now. It will help you breathe more easily.
I'm also giving you some magnesium intravenously. This can help with your heart.
I've also placed an IV in the external jugular vein. This allows quick delivery of meds if needed.
I'll now place you on 4 lead ECG monitoring to continuously monitor your heart. Is that okay?
Alright, go ahead.
We're following the Respiratory Distress Protocol. We will continue monitoring and reassessing.
Heart rate stays high-141 now.
Oxygen saturation is slightly decreased again to 94%.
Blood pressure is 247 over 156.
We'll now perform a 12-lead ECG to look for any cardiac issues.
The 12-lead is back with findings of sinus tachycardia and possible lateral ischemia.
I'm about to apply nitroglycerin paste to your chest to help your heart relax.
Let me know if your breathing feels any different. Better, worse, same?
A little better but still difficult. No chest pain though.
Given your chronic conditions, we're still concerned about pulmonary edema.
Although we have no acute pain, your history still points to a respiratory concern.
We're heading to VCU Health Systems for full evaluation in the resuscitation unit.
Sir, I need to move you to the stretcher now. You'll stay upright with full support.
I just don't want to be laid back.
You'll remain upright in the seat. I've started more oxygen on CPAP and nebulizer again.
Transporting now. I'll give the hospital a pre-arrival report when we arrive.
You were moved with a step chair to the stretcher. Secure in Fowler's position.
Administering more magnesium and keeping nebulizer running. Monitoring for any shifts in vitals.
BP lasted at 240 over 143. HR 137, RR 30. SpO₂ dropped to 93% but still acceptable.
You didn't mention chest pain, right?
No, no chest pain.
Take a deep breath for me.
Still hard, but not as bad.
Hospital's aware of your condition and has a team ready for you.
Updated your vitals with the receiving nurse. Stable despite high BP.
You're in Fowler's position for transport, safely secured.
How are you staying alert right now?
Yeah, I can talk.
Can you tell me when you were born or where you're from?
April 15th, 1941, and I'm from Richmond.
Good, the patient maintains time and place orientation.
Double-checking one more time. IV on the right EJ is active. BP still up. SpO₂ at 93, no chest pain.
Patient escorted to resuscitation bed for immediate care.
Care transferred to facility with full med logs and completed report. Slight delay due to box change.
Transportation and transfers managed swiftly for a critical care transport.
No change reported in chest pain. Let me confirm once again-still no new discomfort?
No, it's all pretty much the same.
Good, still no chest pain reported. Continually monitoring your condition en route.
Final check: BP still elevated at 240/143 but holding steady. Keeping it constant.
